Psychosis, Rebound anxiety, Insomnia, Myoclonus, Epilepsy (seizures). Onset: short-acting 6-12h, long-acting 2-3d.
Rare. Death from SYNERGISTIC resp depression with opioids/alcohol/other sedatives.

Propranolol: lipophilic, CNS (seizures, coma), Na channel block (QRS widening), no ISA. Sotalol = QT prolongation → Torsades.
BB + Class III (K⁺ channel block) → QT prolongation → Torsades. Correct K⁺/Mg²⁺ aggressively. Pacing/isoproterenol for Torsades.

Fluids + glucagon + HIET + vasopressors (NE) → if refractory → mechanical support (ECMO, IABP, Impella).

Often INEFFECTIVE (β₁ blockade, not vagal). Glucagon/HIET/pacing better. May help if vagal component.
Norepinephrine (α₁+β₁) preferred. Avoid pure α (phenylephrine) - may worsen myocardial depression.
Benzos 1st line (lorazepam/diazepam). Avoid phenytoin (Na channel). Phenobarbital/propofol 2nd line.
Observe 6-12h post-normalization. Psych assessment mandatory. Asymptomatic 6h post-ingestion → discharge if low risk.
Hypotension + bradycardia/heart block (L-type Ca²⁺ block → ↓ contractility, ↓ SA/AV conduction). Hyperglycemia (blocks insulin release). Metabolic acidosis.
3g calcium gluconate (30mL 10%) OR 1g calcium chloride (10mL 10%) IV. Repeat q10-20min or infusion to maintain ionized Ca²⁺ > 1.25 mmol/L. FIRST LINE.
Same as BB: insulin 1 U/kg/hr + dextrose (glucose 5-10) + K⁺. Ca²⁺-independent inotropic support.
Verapamil: strongest negative inotropy/chronotropy. Diltiazem intermediate. DHPs (amlodipine) = vascular selective, reflex tachycardia.
Verapamil > diltiazem > amlodipine for cardiac conduction depression. Amlodipine: reflex tachycardia, long t½ (30-50h).
Blocks L-type Ca²⁺ channels in pancreatic β-cells → ↓ insulin release → hyperglycemia.

Vascular selective → profound vasodilation → reflex tachycardia + hypotension. Long t½ 30-50h → observe 24-48h.
Often INEFFECTIVE (SA/AV node Ca²⁺ block, not vagal). Calcium, HIET, pacing better.
FIRST LINE for BB. Adjunct for CCB (less effective). Calcium + HIET primary for CCB.

Until COHb <10% AND asymptomatic. If HBO given, continue O₂ after. Monitor for DNS.

Nitrites cause metHb - avoid if CO (already impaired O₂ delivery). Hydroxocobalamin preferred.

Usually NOT needed (spontaneous reactivation). Atropine for symptoms. May worsen some carbamates.
Edrophonium: improves myasthenic, worsens cholinergic. Clinical: cholinergic = SLUDGE + miosis + bradycardia + fasciculations.
10-20% of total bolus dose required to achieve endpoints per hour. Titrate to endpoints.

Na channel blockade → QRS widening, arrhythmias. Coronary vasospasm → MI (normal coronaries). Myocarditis, cardiomyopathy.
Benzos + nitrates/phentolamine + CCB + aspirin. Avoid beta-blockers. PCI if STEMI.

Days to weeks post-cessation. Low-dose antipsychotic + benzos. Avoid if seizure risk. Cocaine psychosis shorter.

Cocaine: Na channel block (QRS), local anesthetic, shorter. Amph: longer, more psychosis, more serotonergic (MDMA). Both: sympathomimetic.

Red color interferes with COLORIMETRIC assays: creatinine, bilirubin, glucose, COHb, metHb, etc. Use non-colorimetric methods.

front: Intubation in salicylate poisoning - critical principle?
back: Match pre-intubation minute ventilation (high RR, low TV). Avoid permissive hypercapnia - will worsen acidemia catastrophically.
Prevents urine alkalinization (K⁺ shifts into cells in exchange for H⁺). Must replace K⁺ to achieve urine pH 7.5-8.0.
Limited. Salicylate kinetics complex (saturable metabolism, variable Vd, pH-dependent excretion). Do not rely solely on nomogram.
Lower serum levels cause severe toxicity. Often missed. Check level in unexplained confusion/acidosis in elderly on chronic aspirin.

front: Type 1 respiratory failure is classically defined by:?
Which mechanism most commonly causes type 1 respiratory failure?
Which ABG pattern best fits type 1 respiratory failure?
Which of the following is a common cause of type 1 respiratory failure?
Which statement about CO2 in early type 1 respiratory failure is most accurate?
Which condition commonly produces shunt-dominant hypoxemia?
Which test is central to confirming type 1 respiratory failure?
Which of the following best distinguishes type 1 from type 2 respiratory failure?
back: Type 1 has low PaO2 with normal/low PaCO2

front: Poor response of hypoxemia to oxygen may suggest:?
A rising PaCO2 in a patient with type 1 failure suggests:?
A patient with severe pneumonia has an ABG showing PaO2 6.9 kPa, PaCO2 4.3 kPa, and pH 7.47. What is the best interpretation?
A man with sudden dyspnea and pleuritic pain has low oxygen saturation, tachycardia, and a relatively clear chest. Which cause of type 1 respiratory failure should be strongly considered?
A patient with diffuse alveolar infiltrates remains severely hypoxemic despite oxygen. Which mechanism is most likely prominent?
Which immediate step is most appropriate in suspected type 1 respiratory failure?
back: Ignore oxygenation until CT is done

front: A patient with known type 1 respiratory failure now has rising PaCO2 and worsening exhaustion. What is the best interpretation?
Which disease commonly causes type 1 respiratory failure through diffusion impairment?
A patient with acute pulmonary edema has low PaO2 and low-normal PaCO2. What best explains the hypoxemia?
Which statement about type 1 respiratory failure is most correct?
back: It is mainly an oxygenation problem

front: A patient is hypoxemic with a normal chest X-ray but high clinical suspicion of PE. What should be done?
back: Dismiss PE because X-ray is normal

front: In type 1 respiratory failure, the main treatment principle is:?
back: oxygen plus treatment of the underlying cause

front: What is the classic ABG definition of type 1 respiratory failure?
back: PaO2 <8 kPa with normal or low PaCO2.

front: What are the three main mechanisms of type 1 hypoxemia?
Name four common causes of type 1 respiratory failure.?
back: Pneumonia, pulmonary edema, PE, ARDS, ILD.

front: Does type 1 failure usually start as an oxygenation or ventilation problem?
What does a rising PaCO2 in type 1 failure suggest?
Which cause may have a relatively clear chest exam despite severe hypoxemia?
Why may oxygen response be poor in shunt physiology?
back: Blood is passing through non-ventilated alveoli.

front: What is the key contrast with type 2 respiratory failure?
Is treating the cause important in type 1 respiratory failure?
Type 2 respiratory failure is primarily defined by:?
The commonest exam cause of type 2 respiratory failure is:?
In acute hypercapnic respiratory failure, ABG typically shows:?
back: High PaCO2, low pH, near-normal HCO3-

front: The preferred initial oxygen saturation target in a suspected chronic CO2 retainer is usually:?
Which condition most strongly suggests ventilatory pump failure?
A high bicarbonate with elevated PaCO2 usually suggests:?
Which is a contraindication to NIV?
Which test best confirms type 2 respiratory failure?
Failure to improve on NIV should prompt consideration of:?

A woman taking opioids after surgery becomes somnolent with shallow breathing. ABG shows hypercapnia. The most likely mechanism is:?
An obese patient with OSA is hypercapnic with raised bicarbonate and near-normal pH. This most likely indicates:?
A COPD patient on controlled oxygen has repeat ABG showing worsening acidosis and rising PaCO2. The best interpretation is:?
A patient with myasthenia gravis becomes breathless with weak cough and shallow respiration. The investigation most urgently needed is:?
Which feature most strongly supports type 2 rather than type 1 respiratory failure?
A confused patient with hypercapnia is vomiting repeatedly. What is the best ventilatory plan?
A patient with chronic CO2 retention may still have a near-normal pH because of:?
Which bedside clue suggests obesity hypoventilation syndrome?
In acute-on-chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure, ABG typically shows:?
What is the defining gas abnormality in type 2 respiratory failure?
back: Elevated PaCO2 due to inadequate alveolar ventilation, usually >6 kPa.

front: What ABG pattern suggests acute-on-chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure?
back: High PaCO2, high HCO3-, and low pH.

front: Name 4 common causes of type 2 respiratory failure.?
back: COPD exacerbation, obesity hypoventilation, neuromuscular weakness, sedative/opioid toxicity.

front: What are major contraindication clues for NIV?
back: Inability to protect airway, vomiting, facial trauma, severe agitation, peri-arrest state.

front: Why is repeat ABG important after starting treatment?
back: It proves whether ventilation and acidosis are improving or worsening.

front: Child-Pugh B dose adj: High extraction?

LMWH all trimesters; Warf only Mech Mitral 2nd/3rd; DOACs X

Drop 50-60% → Monitor q4wk, Increase dose; Postpartum rapidly reduce

front: Type A vs Type B — one liner?
back: A = Dose-dep, Predictable, 80%; B = Idiosyncratic, Non-dose, Rare, Severe
Stop heparin, Non-heparin anticoag (Argatroban), PF4+SRA, No warfarin until plt>150
Age>40, Ca, HR>120, BSA>10%, Urea>10, Bicarb14 ---
DOV SIM (Define, Objective, Verify, Start, Inform, Monitor)

front: What makes a drug LTI (prescribe by brand)?
back: Narrow TI + High protein binding + Low Vd + Metabolic displacement risk (Phenytoin, Lithium, Warfarin, Tacrolimus, Digoxin, Theophylline)
Statins (Simva, Atorva), CCBs, Tacrolimus, Ciclosporin, DOACs (Riva, Apix), Midazolam, ED drugs, Steroids

front: Which CYP isoform has NO clinically significant inducers?
A patient on warfarin (INR 2.3) is started on fluconazole for oral candidiasis. What is the expected effect on INR and appropriate management?
Which statin is CONTRAINDICATED with clarithromycin?
A renal transplant patient on tacrolimus (trough 8 ng/mL) is started on clarithromycin for pneumonia. What is the immediate management?
back: Increase tacrolimus dose 2x; monitor levels weekly

front: Clopidogrel + Omeprazole interaction is clinically significant because:?
Which of the following is a P-glycoprotein substrate?
The "Triple Whammy" refers to the combination of:?

Mechanism-based (irreversible) CYP inhibition is characterised by:?
Which drug interaction is correctly paired with its monitoring parameter?

A 72-year-old man with atrial fibrillation on warfarin (INR 2.4) develops a lower respiratory tract infection. His GP prescribes clarithromycin 500mg BD. Three days later he presents with epistaxis and an INR of 6.8. He is not bleeding actively. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
back: Stop warfarin; give IV vitamin K 10mg; restart warfarin at same dose when INR <3

front: A 45-year-old woman, 6 months post-renal transplant on tacrolimus (trough 7 ng/mL), develops oral candidiasis. She is prescribed fluconazole 200mg daily. What is the expected effect on tacrolimus levels and appropriate dose adjustment?
back: Tacrolimus levels decrease by 50%; double the tacrolimus dose

front: A 60-year-old man with hypercholesterolaemia on simvastatin 40mg nocte is prescribed clarithromycin 500mg BD for a chest infection. What is the most appropriate action regarding the statin?
A 68-year-old woman with bipolar disorder on lithium carbonate 600mg BD (level 0.7 mmol/L) is started on furosemide 40mg OD for heart failure. What is the most appropriate monitoring plan?
back: Check lithium level in 4 weeks; continue current dose

front: A 28-year-old woman on combined oral contraceptive (ethinylestradiol 30mcg + levonorgestrel 150mcg) is started on rifampicin 600mg OD for tuberculosis. What is the effect on contraceptive efficacy and appropriate advice?
back: No significant interaction; continue OCP as normal

front: A 55-year-old man on digoxin 125mcg OD for atrial fibrillation (level 1.0 ng/mL) is started on amiodarone 200mg OD for paroxysmal AF. What is the expected effect on digoxin levels and management?
back: Digoxin levels decrease by 30%; increase digoxin dose

front: A 40-year-old man with epilepsy on phenytoin 300mg OD (total level 15 µg/mL, albumin 38 g/L) is started on sodium valproate 500mg BD for adjunctive therapy. Two weeks later he develops nystagmus and ataxia. Total phenytoin level is 16 µg/mL. What is the most likely explanation?
back: Valproate induces phenytoin metabolism causing therapeutic failure

front: A 75-year-old woman on ramipril 10mg OD, furosemide 40mg OD, and metformin 1g BD for type 2 diabetes develops osteoarthritis and is started on ibuprofen 400mg TDS by her GP. One week later she presents with confusion and reduced urine output. U&Es: Na 138, K 5.8, Urea 22, Creatinine 280 (baseline 110). What is the primary mechanism of this acute kidney injury?

A 30-year-old man with schizophrenia on quetiapine 300mg BD is prescribed ciprofloxacin 500mg BD for a urinary tract infection. He has a history of borderline prolonged QTc (460ms). What is the most appropriate action?
back: Continue both; monitor ECG in 1 week

front: A 50-year-old woman on rivaroxaban 20mg OD (CrCl 45 mL/min) for DVT treatment is diagnosed with invasive pulmonary aspergillosis and started on voriconazole 200mg BD IV. What is the most appropriate anticoagulation management?
back: Continue rivaroxaban 20mg; monitor anti-Xa levels daily

front: What is the effect of rifampicin on CYP enzymes?
back: Broad inducer (1A2, 2B6, 2C8, 2C9, 2C19, 3A4, P-gp, UGT, OATP) — assume interaction with EVERY drug
CYP3A4 inhibition (R-warfarin) + CYP2C9 inhibition (S-warfarin) + Mechanism-based inhibition + ↓ Gut Vit K production
Reduce warfarin 20-30%, Monitor INR 2-3x/week during + 1 week after
Reduce tacrolimus 66-75%, Monitor trough levels q2-3d + Renal function q2-3d
CONTRAINDICATED. Switch to Rosuvastatin/Pravastatin or hold simvastatin
Omeprazole = Strong CYP2C19 inhibitor → ↓ Clopidogrel active metabolite → ↑ Stent thrombosis/MI risk
Digoxin, Tacrolimus, Ciclosporin, Sirolimus, DOACs, HIV PIs
Li level weekly × 4, then monthly. Target 0.4-0.6 mmol/L. Counsel re: dehydration.
Halve digoxin dose. Check level at 1 week (≥6h post-dose). Monitor K⁺, ECG.

front: Phenytoin + Valproate — what level to monitor?
back: FREE phenytoin level (target 1-2 µg/mL). Total level misleading due to displacement.

front: DOAC + Ketoconazole — Dabigatran vs Rivaroxaban vs Apixaban?
back: Dabi: AVOID. Riva: Reduce 15mg OD (CrCl>30). Apix: Reduce 2.5mg BD (if 2/3 criteria).
Oral CYP3A4 substrates with HIGH first-pass metabolism (Simvastatin, Felodipine, Tacrolimus). IV drugs unaffected.
Additional barrier contraception during + 4 weeks after stopping rifampicin.
Onset: Days (new enzyme synthesis). Offset: Days-weeks (enzyme turnover ~72h).

front: Protein binding displacement — clinically significant for?
back: High extraction, narrow TI drugs: Warfarin, Phenytoin, Valproate.
QTc >500ms or ΔQTc >60ms from baseline → Stop offending drugs, correct K⁺/Mg²⁺, Cardiology review. Torsades → IV Magnesium 2g bolus, overdrive pacing, isoprenaline. ---
Consider stop if Frail/LE/ADR/Pref; Continue secondary ---

back: Baseline hormone + paired tropic hormone (e.g., TSH + fT4)

back: Only unbound hormone is biologically active

front: A 30-year-old woman on combined OCP has total T4 180 nmol/L (ref 60-160), TSH 1.5 mIU/L. Explanation?

back: PNMT converts noradrenaline to adrenaline in adrenal medulla

front: Nephrotic syndrome: low total T4, normal TSH. Why?

front: Checkpoint inhibitor-induced hypophysitis most commonly affects which cell type?

front: ICI endocrinopathy monitoring baseline and on-treatment:?
back: TSH, fT4, cortisol (8am), ACTH, glucose, HbA1c q4-6wk

front: 60yo man on pembrolizumab for melanoma: week 8, fatigue, nausea, hypotension, hyponatraemia. AM cortisol 80 nmol/L, ACTH undetectable. TSH 0.1, fT4 8. Management?
back: Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q6h → prednisolone 1-2mg/kg; hold pembrolizumab; thyroxine after cortisol replacement

front: 55yo woman on ipilimumab+nivolumab: week 10, neck pain, thyrotoxic symptoms. TSH <0.01, fT4 45, fT3 12, ESR 60. Thyroid uptake low. Next?
45yo man on nivolumab: new-onset polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, glucose 28, ketones 4+, pH 7.15, C-peptide 0.1, GAD65+. Diagnosis?
70yo woman on atezolizumab: routine bloods week 12: TSH 18, fT4 8, asymptomatic. Antibodies negative. Management?

Secondary hyperparathyroidism in CKD: primary driver is:?
back: Phosphate retention → ↓ ionised Ca²⁺ → PTH ↑ + ↓ Vit D synthesis
Abnormal Ca/P/PTH/Vit D, Bone abnormalities, Vascular calcification

front: KDIGO target for PTH in CKD Stage 3-5 (not on dialysis):?

Time (6-12mo); cinacalcet; parathyroidectomy if severe hypercalcaemia

front: CKD Stage 4 (eGFR 25): phosphate 1.9, calcium 2.2, PTH 350 (ULN 65), 25-OH vit D 30. Management?
back: Cholecalciferol 1000-2000 IU/day + dietary phosphate restriction + phosphate binder if needed

front: Dialysis patient: PTH 800, phosphate 2.2, calcium 2.5 on calcium acetate. Next?
back: Switch to non-calcium binder (sevelamer) + consider cinacalcet

front: CKD Stage 5D: adynamic bone disease suspected (PTH 80, low turnover on biopsy). Fracture risk?
back: Avoid oversuppression of PTH; target PTH 2-9x ULN; minimise calcium load

front: Post-renal transplant (3 months): calcium 2.7, PTH 180, eGFR 45. Management?
back: Observe 6-12 months; Usually resolves; if persistent hypercalcaemia → cinacalcet/parathyroidectomy

front: CKD patient on cinacalcet: symptomatic hypocalcaemia. Action?
Thyroid function in healthy ageing: typical pattern:?
back: TSH ↑ (upper reference limit increases), fT4 normal

front: Subclinical hypothyroidism in elderly (>80): TSH 6-10, asymptomatic. Management?
back: Observe + repeat in 3-6 months (no routine treatment if TSH <10)

front: Levothyroxine dosing in elderly: start low go slow. Typical starting dose:?
Adrenal insufficiency in elderly: atypical presentation:?
back: Fatigue, anorexia, weight loss, postural hypotension, hyponatraemia (often no hyperpigmentation)

front: SIADH in elderly: most common drug causes:?

back: 4-6 mmol/L/24h (max 8 in first 24h if severe symptoms)

front: Diabetes in elderly: HbA1c target (frail, multi-morbidity):?

back: Oral bisphosphonate (alendronate) + Ca/Vit D; denosumab if contraindicated
Hypocalcaemia (ensure Vit D replete); stop → rapid bone loss (rebound vertebral #)

front: 85yo woman: falls, fatigue, TSH 8.5, fT4 12, no goitre, no cardiac history. Best approach?
back: Repeat TSH/fT4 in 3 months; treat if TSH >10 or symptomatic

front: 80yo man on sertraline: confused, Na+ 118, euvolaemic, urine osm 400, urine Na+ 60. Management?
back: Fluid restriction <1L + stop sertraline → consider tolvaptan if severe

front: 78yo woman: T2DM, eGFR 38, HbA1c 8.2% on metformin 1g BD. Best adjustment?
back: Reduce metformin to 500mg BD (eGFR 30-45); review HbA1c target 7.5-8.0%

front: 82yo man: hip fracture, DEXA T-score -3.0, eGFR 45. First-line?
back: Alendronate 70mg weekly + Ca/Vit D; dental review

front: 90yo woman: community-acquired pneumonia, on prednisolone 5mg for PMR. Acute illness: stress-dose steroids?
back: Hydrocortisone 50mg IV q6h (if AI suspected) or double oral dose; taper after recovery

front: hCG cross-reactivity with TSH receptor causes:?
back: Transient gestational thyrotoxicosis (1st trimester), TSH suppressed, fT4 ↑

front: Levothyroxine dose adjustment in pregnant hypothyroid woman:?

front: Graves disease in pregnancy: antithyroid drug of choice:?

Medical nutrition therapy + glucose monitoring; insulin if targets not met
75g OGTT at 6-12 weeks; then q1-3 years (lifelong T2DM risk 50% at 10y)

front: 32yo woman, 8 weeks pregnant, known hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100µg. TSH 4.5 (ref 0.1-2.5). Action?

front: 28yo woman, 10 weeks: Graves disease, on carbimazole 15mg. TSH <0.01, fT4 35. Best switch?
back: Switch to PTU 100-200mg/day (1st trimester); plan to switch back to carbimazole at 16 weeks

front: 26yo woman, 26 weeks: GDM diagnosed (fasting 5.8, 1h 11.2, 2h 9.8). Diet-controlled. Targets?

front: Postpartum thyroiditis at 3 months: symptomatic thyrotoxicosis (palpitations, tremor). Management?

RET proto-oncogene (10q11); gain-of-function mutations (Cys 634 for 2A, M918T for 2B)

CASR inactivating mutation (loss of function) → ↑ Ca²⁺ set-point

front: Neonatal diabetes mellitus (transient) most common gene:?

GNAS mutation (maternal imprinting) → Albright hereditary osteodystrophy + multi-hormone resistance

front: 30yo woman: hypercalcaemia, PTH 12, urinary calcium/creatinine clearance ratio 0.01. Mother similar. Gene?

Severe insulin resistance → diabetes, hypertriglyceridaemia, NAFLD, PCOS

front: Generalized lipodystrophy with uncontrolled diabetes (HbA1c 10%), triglycerides 25mmol/L. Add to insulin?

front: 30yo transmasculine patient: on testosterone gel 50mg/day for 6 months. Hb 185, Hct 56%. Management?

front: 22yo transmasculine: requests hysterectomy/oophorectomy. Testosterone stopped 4 weeks pre-op?

front: Non-binary patient: low-dose testosterone (microdosing) for partial masculinisation. Monitoring?
back: Same parameters (testosterone, Hb/Hct, LFTs, lipids) but targets individualised

front: Pituitary apoplexy with improving vision post-steroids. Next?
